title: A Marker on the Freedom Trail
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Summit (Argo), Illinois, in May 2011, Rev. Wheeler Parker Jr. opens with the recent unveiling of the Emmett Till marker at Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market, one of about 25 planned Mississippi Freedom Trail markers, a moving event right down the road from where he once lived that recalled where they were in 1955 and what remains to be done.

title: Rich Delta Soil and Fifty People
summary: Parker, who moved to Argo outside Chicago in 1947 but returns to Mississippi about twice a year, describes getting off the train in Money in the summer of 1955, a tiny place of maybe fifty people on rich Delta soil where the boys grabbed cotton sacks for the fields, and recalls his grandfather Papa, a no-nonsense man of means respected for carrying himself with dignity.

title: The Shotgun House on Dark Fear Road
summary: He describes his easygoing grandmother and his schoolteacher mother, the brand-new shotgun house next to his grandmother's on Dark Fear Road near a body of water that flooded under the house, the neighboring sawmill with its screaming saw, the surrounding cotton fields, and the one-room church school where a single teacher kept order and once sent his uncle out for switches.

title: You Could Not Pick Cotton on Cornflakes
summary: Parker recalls moving up to the uptown school with real classrooms, the church cemetery where Emmett's grave would later be dug, the few uptown stores, and a farm world with no fast food where you ate rabbit, chicken, and cornbread to work the field, and where children started school only after the cotton was in, so a boy might be eighteen finishing eighth grade.

title: Going Behind the Iron Curtain
topic: Awareness of Danger and the First Days
summary: He explains that everyone heard the stories and knew exactly where they were going, the South equated with going behind the Iron Curtain to Russia, a place with no protection under the law, yet the friends had so much fun with nothing in those first days, picking cotton half the day in the late-August heat, swimming where snakes were said not to bite, raiding watermelon patches, and riding around with his uncle Jai, while remaining aware of what could happen there.

title: Then Emmett Whistled
summary: He describes riding three miles to the little town on Wednesday near the end of the day, going into the store to buy something and feeling apprehension when Emmett came in, then Emmett and Simeon Wright leaving and Emmett whistling at Carolyn Bryant Donham as she came out, an act so out of the blue that Parker says even now they do not know what possessed a boy with no idea of the danger.

title: Dust Flying on the Gravel Road
summary: Parker recounts the boys rushing to the crowded car when someone said the woman was getting a gun, Emmett urging his uncle to drive while they found a dropped cigarette, the terror when a car appeared behind them in the dust on the gravel road, leaping out to hide and falling among the cotton bolls as the car passed, then regrouping while a girl with them warned that she knew those people and it was not over.

title: A Dog and a Boy Who Loved Animals
summary: He describes how the incident was not taken seriously and went unmentioned through Friday and into Saturday, the big shopping day when everyone crowded into Greenwood and Johnson Street, a stop at a juke joint called Four Fifths, and the drive home when his uncle Maurice ran over a dog and Emmett, fond of animals, started crying, revealing his sensitive side.

title: He Was Leo the Lion
summary: Parker reflects on Emmett, his next-door neighbor in Chicago, as a prankster whose idea of funny was not always funny, who once announced on a street that his two big cousins from Argo could beat anybody when Chicago gangs were dangerous, a natural center of attraction Parker calls Leo the lion, never angry or mean-spirited but always doing something to get attention, and explains the age distinction between a sixteen-year-old and the fourteen-year-old who looked up to him as the upperclassman.

title: We're Looking for a Fat Boy From Chicago
topic: The Men Arrive in the Night
summary: Parker describes the large house with its screened-in porch and four big rooms, getting to bed around midnight in pitch darkness, and waking around two-thirty to men talking about the store and saying they were looking for a fat boy from Chicago, which sent him into pure terror and prayer, recalling a World War II film of a man promising God he would change if he survived a shark.

title: A Pistol in One Hand, a Flashlight in the Other
summary: He recounts lying shaking like a leaf, terrified of guns since a childhood incident, as a man with a pistol and flashlight walked past him and his sleeping cousins Curtis and Maurice Wright into the third room where Emmett slept, roused him while Emmett tried to put his socks on not knowing who he was dealing with, the men fierce and, in hindsight, likely drinking.

title: The Protection Is Gone
summary: Parker describes hearing the angry men, learning later his grandmother had tried to intercede, and the unbearable terror after both grandparents left and his grandmother was taken to her brother's house, leaving him certain the men, Milam and Bryant, would return, so he put his shoes on and waited alone through the night, never waking his uncle, planning to flee into the woods.

title: They Said They'd Send Me Up There Too
summary: He recalls his courageous cousin Curtis calling for help, his mother getting involved, the calls being made, someone in the crowd joking they would send him up there to get him because they still did not believe Emmett would be killed, and how, sleepless for over twenty-four hours, he was finally put on a train through Memphis to Chicago, scared again at the washroom signs.

title: Go Hold Your Mother
topic: Return to Chicago and Mamie Till
summary: Parker describes reaching Mamie Till-Mobley's house at 64th and St. Lawrence where the family sat grieving before the body was found, being told to go hold his mother, returning to Argo as if to a nightmare, and later being shaken to the bone seeing a jeweler named JP who looked just like one of the jurors, an encounter that he says changed his whole life.

title: I Showed No Remorse at the Funeral
summary: He reflects on how his religious upbringing taught him to exhale and let trouble go, and on the funeral, where he showed no remorse or sadness because he believed he would see Emmett again, though he weeps telling it now, describing a solemn crowd filled with disbelief that it could happen to a child, since children were for the most part exempt, referencing the book Without Sanctuary.

title: Six Years to Keep a Promise
topic: Conversion and the Promise to God
summary: Parker recounts visiting Mamie by bus as she went on with her life, earned a college degree, and remarried, and explains that the promise he made to God that terrifying night stayed with him until at twenty-two, six years later, he finally kept it and changed, finding peace and happiness with no regrets across what is now fifty years.

title: His Granddaddy Was a Slave
summary: He describes returning to Mississippi around age twenty-six, cautious about where he stopped, visiting the uncle who sheltered his grandmother that night and now lived past a hundred, and another uncle whose grandfather had been a slave, and reflects on his grandparents adjusting to Chicago, his grandfather who had planned to die in the South taking up farming near the Teal center.

title: An Idea Whose Time Had Come
summary: Parker recalls that in 1985 Rich Samuels of NBC, who met Mamie supporting the family of slain basketball player Ben Wilson, came and did a documentary on the Till case, the first real interest after years when people felt the boy got what he deserved, and how from there others began coming, an idea whose time had come, even as Parker insists on telling history truthfully rather than embellishing it.

title: The Law Cannot Legislate the Heart
summary: Asked about Reverend King's 1966 visit to Chicago, Parker, just back from the Army and starting his business, remembers the hate and animosity and people saying King should go back south, and articulates the conviction that recurs through his testimony, that the law can make you behave within a certain realm but cannot legislate what is deep in the heart.

title: Alvin Sykes and the Till Bill
topic: The Push to Reopen the Case
summary: Parker credits the unpretentious Alvin Sykes, who came with one reason to get the case opened after a Kansas case, with a pivotal role, recounting how they met with Eric Holder, who called Sykes a legend in his own time, and how Sykes pursued the Till Bill that President George W. Bush signed, allotting eleven million dollars a year for ten years to pursue civil rights cases dating back from 1970.
related_events: Emmett Till Unsolved Civil Rights Crime Act

title: All the White Men in Mississippi
summary: On the 2005 reopening by the Justice Department and Mississippi officials, Parker describes moving from a pessimistic view that white men in Mississippi would never open the case, to cautious hope when he learned a woman, then a Black woman, would lead it, and acknowledges the FBI doing an investigation it would not have done fifty years earlier, while he remained a realist about the country's attitude.

title: I Just Want to Know What Happened
summary: Parker recounts the FBI taking the case to a grand jury that declined to indict Carolyn Bryant Donham, and the man from Ohio whose son sought immunity claiming he was not there, and insists he wants not jail for anyone but closure and the facts of what happened, which Carolyn Bryant Donham knows, reflecting too on a tense visit to Mississippi and on dealing always with human beings who can only go so far.

title: All I Did Was Survive
summary: Parker finds the deepest meaning in telling history so children can benefit, paying his own way to the marker ceremony so the story gets out, recalling a friend, Mike Smolow of Dominican High School, who was a white pallbearer at Mamie's funeral, and refusing to be made a hero since all he did was survive, scared, to tell the story that people deeply appreciate hearing truthfully.

title: Markers by the River and the Barn
summary: He describes the designated stretch of highway, presented to the body by a senator and torn down many times, and the Tallahatchie County effort that placed markers at the riverside where the body came out, at the funeral home, at the Sumner courthouse they hope to restore, and at the barn where Emmett was tortured, things Parker says are good for history even when some people take advantage of them.

title: You Cannot Change a Die-Hard Heart
summary: Parker assesses the people working to right wrongs as sincere but bound to be frustrated because such efforts do not change hearts, warns against expecting more than they will get amid what he sees as a revival of racism, and urges them to know where they are and keep changing what change can be made, slowly, hoping God touches people's hearts.

title: The Fire in Somebody's Belly
summary: Parker closes on finding younger people more receptive than college students he met at the University of Missouri, telling both that progress always required someone, often a white person, to put the fire in their belly and stand up, citing Lincoln, Kennedy, and Johnson signing the Civil Rights Bill, urging Black and white youth to work together since the young will have to make the changes the case still demands fifty-seven years later.
